coredata.dc:title: Cascade amplification strategy combined with analyte-triggered fluorescence switching of dual-quenching system for highly sensitive detection of isoniazide
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zuo, Ya-Nan
Xia, Yinghui
Li, Yanyu
Sun, Jing
Zhao, Xian-En
Zhu, Shuyun
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive fluorescence sensing platform consisting of manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as dual nanoquenchers has been constructed to detect isoniazid combined with analyte-triggered cascade reactions. The fluorescence of 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) is quenched simultaneously by MnO2 and AuNPs via inner filter effect. MnO2 is decomposed by isoniazid to generate Mn2+, which makes AuNPs aggregated. The quenching abilities of both the decomposed MnO2 and aggregated AuNPs are inhibited, causing remarkable fluorescence recovery. The usage of dual nanoquenchers enhances the quenching efficiency and reduces the fluorescence background. Moreover, the isoniazid-triggered cascade reaction further amplifies the readout signal. Thus, this strategy exhibits higher sensitivity towards the detection of isoniazid. Compared with MnO2-based fluorescence assay, this strategy possesses lower limit of detection. This strategy has been successfully used to detect isoniazid in pharmaceutical preparations, which is of great significance for drug analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-nanoquenchers
Cascade reaction
Signal amplification
Inner filter effect
Isoniazid

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of AuNPs and MnO2 nanosheets
Establishment of cascade amplification strategy for detection of isoniazid and sensing mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) and XRD pattern (B) of AuNPs. TEM image (C) of MnO2 nanosheets and XPS spectra of Mn 2p (D).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of DAP under different conditions with the excitation wavelength of 418 nm: DAP (a), DAP + MnO2 (b), DAP + MnO2 + isoniazid (c), DAP + AuNPs + MnO2 (d), DAP + AuNPs + MnO2 + isoniazid (e). [DAP] = 2 μM, [isoniazid] = 100 μM, V(MnO2) = 50 μL, V(AuNPs) = 120 μL. (B) The TEM image of AuNPs-MnO2 nanosheets incubated with isoniazid. (C) The UV–vis spectra of MnO2 and AuNPs and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of DAP. (D) PL decay of DAP without and with MnO2, AuNPs, MnO2 + AuNPs.

The effects of MnO2 volumes (A) and AuNPs amounts (B) on the fluorescence intensities of DAP. The effects of pH (C) and reaction time of isoniazid with MnO2 (D) on the (F-F 0)/F 0 of DAP-MnO2-AuNPs system, where F and F 0 is the fluorescence of the DAP-MnO2-AuNPs system with and without isoniazid, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of DAP-MnO2-AuNPs system with varied concentrations of isoniazid (0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 2, 3.5, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 μM). (B) The relationship between fluorescence recovery efficiency (F-F 0)/F 0 and isoniazid concentrations. where F and F 0 is the fluorescence of the DAP-MnO2-AuNPs system without and with isoniazid, respectively. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of DAP-MnO2 system with different concentrations of isoniazid (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5, 15, 30, 50, and 100 μM). (D) The relationship between (F-F 0)/F 0 and isoniazid concentrations, where F and F 0 is the fluorescence of the DAP-MnO2 system with and without isoniazid, respectively. Error bars represented the standard deviation (n = 3).

The (F-F 0)/F 0 of DAP-MnO2-AuNPs system with some interfering compounds and isoniazid. From a-k were Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cl-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, SO4 2-, histidine, lysine, alanine, glucose, lactose, sucrose, starch, urea, and isoniazid, respectively. [each analyte] = 100 μM.
Mechanism of fluorescence detection of isoniazid based on cascade amplification strategy combined with dual nanoquenchers.
Comparisons of analytical performance for isoniazid based on different methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: a coordination polymer formed by cobalt(III) tetrapyridylporphyrin species, four ruthenium dicarboxybipyridine complexes and nickel(II) ions.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: nitrogen-doped carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: One tablet labeled to contain 100 mg of isoniazid.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean value found of three different sample solutions.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Theoretical values for t-Test (0.05) and F-Test (0.05).

head.ce:title: Cascade amplification strategy combined with analyte-triggered fluorescence switching of dual-quenching system for highly sensitive detection of isoniazide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu City 273165, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu City 273165, Shandong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qinghai Key Laboratory of Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining City 810001, Qinghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qinghai Key Laboratory of Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qinghai Key Laboratory of Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining City 810001, Qinghai, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and sensitive IFE-based fluorescence assay for isoniazid has been developed with manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as dual nanoquenchers in combination of analyte-triggered cascade reactions for the first time.

A sensitive fluorescence sensing platform consisting of manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as dual nanoquenchers has been constructed to detect isoniazid combined with analyte-triggered cascade reactions. The fluorescence of 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) is quenched simultaneously by MnO2 and AuNPs via inner filter effect. MnO2 is decomposed by isoniazid to generate Mn2+, which makes AuNPs aggregated. The quenching abilities of both the decomposed MnO2 and aggregated AuNPs are inhibited, causing remarkable fluorescence recovery. The usage of dual nanoquenchers enhances the quenching efficiency and reduces the fluorescence background. Moreover, the isoniazid-triggered cascade reaction further amplifies the readout signal. Thus, this strategy exhibits higher sensitivity towards the detection of isoniazid. Compared with MnO2-based fluorescence assay, this strategy possesses lower limit of detection. This strategy has been successfully used to detect isoniazid in pharmaceutical preparations, which is of great significance for drug analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence sensors have raised overwhelming concern owing to the advantages of extraordinary sensitivity, quick response, and easy operation [1]. Various sensing mechanisms have major roles to establish fluorescence assays [2,3]. Inner filter effect (IFE) is a phenomenon in which the excited light or/and emitted light of a fluorophore is absorbed by an absorbent. Thus, the absorption spectrum of the absorbent is required to overlap with the excitation/emission spectrum of fluorophores. IFE exhibits high sensitivity by transforming the absorbance value of the absorbent into the exponential change in fluorescence intensity [4,5]. In addition, IFE does not need specific distance between the fluorescent donor and the receptor. Therefore, it is convenient and flexible to realize IFE without requiring additional surface modification.

With the rapid development of nanotechnology in analytical chemistry, various nanoquenchers have been applied in the construction of IFE-based fluorescence sensors. Among them, two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials have advantages with great light harvesting ability and high surface area [6,7]. Manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2), as an emerging 2D nanomaterial, has drawn wide attention because of high molar extinction coefficient, simple preparation, and easy access to raw materials. Moreover, the broad absorption within 200–600 nm overlaps with fluorescence spectrum of most fluorophores [8,9]. Therefore, a series of IFE-based fluorescence assays with MnO2 as an efficient nanoquencher have been designed for the detection of enzymes [10,11], biomolecules [12], metal ions [13], nucleic acids [14], pesticides [15] and so on. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), as another popular nanoquencher, has been applied to design IFE-based fluorescence sensors owing to the peculiarities of high molar extinction coefficient, broad absorption spectrum and good biocompatibility [16–18]. However, fluorescence assays based on single nanoquencher is subject to strong background interference. Moreover, the low quenching efficiency of single nanoquencher limits the sensitivity. The usage of double nanoquenchers may significantly reduce the background to the minimum value and can improve the detection sensitivity [19].

Isoniazid, as one of the effective first-line anti-tuberculosis drugs, is widely used in tuberculosis prevention and chemotherapy because of its high efficiency, good tolerance, and low toxicity [20,21]. However, excessive dosage may cause some side effects, including endocrine disorders, thrombocytopenia, etc. Thus, it is significant to design a simple, selective, and sensitive strategy to detect isoniazid for clinical treatment and pharmacological research. So far, different techniques have been used to detect isoniazid such as titrimetry [22], colorimetry [23], electrochemical techniques [24], flow-injection chemiluminescence method [25], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [26], etc. So far, there are few reports on fluorescence detection of isoniazid. For example, Ma and coworkers designed a fluorescence assay for isoniazid based on MnO2-tetramethylbenzidine-carbon dots system [27]. Zhao's group established a signal-on fluorescence assay for isoniazid with silver nanoclusters and MnO2 [21]. However, these methods exhibit low sensitivity, inhibiting their practical application.

Herein, a highly sensitive fluorescence assay for isoniazid has been designed with MnO2 and AuNPs as dual nanoquenchers (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence of 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) can be simultaneously quenched by MnO2 and AuNPs via IFE. The addition of isoniazid can decompose the MnO2 into Mn2+, which makes AuNPs aggregated. The quenching abilities of both the decomposed MnO2 and aggregated AuNPs are inhibited, causing remarkable fluorescence recovery. The usage of dual nanoquenchers enhances the quenching efficiency and reduces the fluorescence background. Moreover, the isoniazid-triggered cascade reaction further amplifies the readout signal. Thus, this strategy exhibits higher sensitivity towards the detection of isoniazid. Compared with MnO2-based fluorescence assay, the limit of detection (LOD) of this strategy is much lower. This strategy has been successfully used to detect isoniazid in pharmaceutical preparations, which is of great significance for drug analysis.
The materials and apparatus were provided in the Supporting Information.

According to our previous work [28], the preparation procedure for MnO2 is as follows: 4.4 g TMA·OH, 20 mL H2O2 (30%), and 10 mL MnCl2·4H2O (0.3 M) were mixed thoroughly and stirred at room temperature overnight. Then, the precipitate was washed with CH3OH and distilled water for three times, respectively. Finally, the bulk MnO2 nanosheets were diluted with 100 mL secondary distilled water for use.

Based on the literature [29], AuNPs were prepared as follows: 50 mL 1.0 mM HAuCl4 solution was heated to boiling and stirred intensively followed by the introduction of the solution containing 0.02 g sodium citrate and 0.001 g CA. After keeping boiling for 1 min, the mixture was cooled to obtain the citrate-stabilized AuNPs.

For isoniazid detection, 50 μL MnO2 nanosheets solution and 50 μL different concentrations isoniazid were mixed well. After 5 min, 120 μL AuNPs were added into the above mixture. After reaction for 5 min, 10 μL DAP (0.1 mM) and 280 μL PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 6.0) were added and reacted for 5 min. The fluorescence emission spectra from 500 to 700 nm were recorded under the excitation of 418 nm.

To detect isoniazid in tablet, one isoniazid tablet was weighted and ground to powder. 99.05 mg powder was dissolved with 100 mL water. After 20 min’s ultrasonic treatment, the solution was filtered to obtain stock solution containing 5 mM isoniazid. Lastly, the sample was analyzed by this strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of AuNPs and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 A presents the TEM imaging of AuNPs. AuNPs are monodispersed spherical with an average diameter of 13.7 nm. Fig. 1B shows the XRD pattern obtained for AuNPs. The diffraction peaks are obtained at 2θ values of 38.29°, 44.52°, 64.78° and 77.67°, corresponding to (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of fcc lattice of metallic gold, respectively. These prominent Bragg reflections agree well with the standard AuNPs (JCPDS No. 03–065-2870). The morphology of MnO2 nanosheets was characterized by TEM. As exhibited in Fig. 1C, MnO2 possess an ultrathin plane with occasional crinkles. The XPS analysis indicates the existence of Mn element (Fig. 1D). Two characteristic peaks are obtained at 653.4 and 641.6 eV corresponding to Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2, respectively, which are ascribed to oxidation state for Mn(IV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of cascade amplification strategy for detection of isoniazid and sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the feasibility of this strategy, fluorescence spectra of DAP under different conditions were recorded. As presented in Fig. 2 A, DAP exhibits a strong fluorescence with emission peak centered at 570 nm (curve a). After the addition of MnO2, 70% of the fluorescence is quenched, indicating the efficient quenching ability of MnO2 nanosheets (curve b). On intruding isoniazid, the fluorescence recovers obviously (curve c) and the fluorescence recovery efficiency (F-F 0)/F 0 is 180% ascribed to the efficient reaction of MnO2 with isoniazid, where F and F 0 is the fluorescence intensity of DAP-MnO2 system with and without isoniazid, respectively. As an ideal quencher, AuNPs can also quench 30% fluorescence of DAP (Fig. S1). However, the fluorescence recovery efficiency of DAP is relatively low after the introduction of isoniazid, indicating weak interaction between AuNPs and isoniazid (Fig. S1). When both MnO2 and AuNPs are added to the DAP solution simultaneously, up to 88% of fluorescence is quenched remarkably attributed to the combined effect of MnO2 and AuNPs (curve d, Fig. 2). After introduction of isoniazid into DAP-MnO2-AuNPs system, the fluorescence of DAP recovers dramatically and the fluorescence recovery efficiency reaches up to 558%. It is obvious that the usage of double quenchers improves the detection sensitivity remarkably. Moreover, Fig. 2B shows the effect of isoniazid on MnO2-AuNPs system via TEM analysis. As expected, the monodispersed AuNPs exhibit an aggregation state, which is also facile for the fluorescence recovery of DAP. In contrast, there is no obvious change for the fluorescence of DAP in the presence of either isoniazid or Mn2+ (Fig. S2). Meanwhile, the fluorescence of DAP-AuNPs system recovers gradually as Mn2+ concentration increases (Fig. S3). Thus, we deduce that the highly efficient fluorescence recovery ascribes to decomposition of MnO2 triggered by isoniazid into Mn2+, which then induces the aggregation of AuNPs. Ascribed to the analyte triggered cascade reaction, this amplified strategy exhibits high sensitivity towards isoniazid.

Then, the sensing mechanism was investigated in detail. Firstly, the reaction of isoniazid and MnO2 was proved by UV–vis spectra and TEM imaging. Fig. S4A shows that the typical absorption peak of MnO2 locates at 380 nm resulting from the d-d transition of Mn(IV). When isoniazid concentration increases, the absorbance at 380 nm decreases gradually until it disappears. In addition, TEM image shows that the sheet structure of MnO2 nanosheets is destroyed by isoniazid (Fig. S4B). According to the previous reports, MnO2 nanosheets is decomposed by isoniazid to generate Mn2+ [21,27]. The influences of Mn2+ on AuNPs were studied with UV–vis spectra and TEM. Fig. S4C shows that AuNPs display an absorption peak at 520 nm with a typical wine-red color. When Mn2+ is added into AuNPs solution, the absorbance at 520 nm decreases obviously and a new peak at 680 nm ascribed to the aggregated AuNPs emerges followed by the color changing to blue. TEM imaging also proves the aggregation of AuNPs with Mn2+ (Fig. S4D). As expected, MnO2 is decomposed by isoniazid into Mn2+, which induces the aggregation of AuNPs. Owing to isoniazid-triggered cascade reaction, the fluorescence of DAP restores remarkably.

The quenching mechanism were investigated in detail. As presented in Fig. 2C, the absorption spectrum of MnO2 overlaps with both excitation and emission spectra of DAP while the absorption spectrum of AuNPs partially overlaps with the emission spectrum of DAP. The zeta potentials of DAP, MnO2 nanosheets and AuNPs are found to be −1.11, −2.47, and −1.45 mV, respectively (Fig. S5). Thus, there is no electronic attraction between them, and their distance is far. Furthermore, the fluorescent lifetime (τ) measurements were performed to investigate their interaction. Fig. 2D shows that the fluorescence decay profiles of DAP changes negligibly with either MnO2 nanosheets or AuNPs. The τ of DAP without and with MnO2 nanosheets, AuNPs, MnO2 nanosheets and AuNPs is 1.091, 1.097, 1.107 and 1.105 ns, respectively. All the above results agree with the elements required for inner filter effect (IFE). Therefore, an ultrasensitive IFE-based fluorescence strategy for sensing isoniazid can be readily achieved with MnO2 nanosheets and AuNPs as dual quenchers combined with cascade amplification strategy.

To obtain an excellent isoniazid detection performance, the experimental parameters such as the volume of MnO2 nanosheets, amounts of AuNPs, pH as well as incubation time were optimized. Fig. 3 A shows that the fluorescence of DAP decreases obviously as MnO2 volume increases and then slowly when the MnO2 volume exceeds 50 μL. Excessive MnO2 nanosheets is not facile for fluorescence recovery. Therefore, 50 μL MnO2 nanosheets solution was selected. Fig. 3B shows the effects of amount of AuNPs solution on the fluorescence of DAP-AuNPs system. The intensity decreases gradually until balance when the amount of AuNPs solution increases from 0 to 120 μL, which is the optimal amount of AuNPs solution. pH is a crucial factor that influences the luminescent properties of DAP. Therefore, the effect of pH on the response of isoniazid was studied. Fig. 3C shows that (F-F 0)/F 0 increases firstly and then decreases as pH increases. The maximum (F-F 0)/F 0 is obtained at pH 5.0. However, the fluorescence of DAP at pH 5.0 is not strong (Fig. S6). Thus, pH 6.0 is selected to obtain both the high (F-F 0)/F 0 and strong fluorescence signal. Fig. 3D shows the influence of reaction time of isoniazid with MnO2 on the response of isoniazid. When time increases from 1 to 15 min, (F-F 0)/F 0 changes slightly. In consideration of complete reaction at low concentration, 5 min is chosen in the next experiments.

Under the optimal conditions, the sensitivity of DAP-MnO2-AuNPs system towards isoniazid were investigated. Fig. 4 A shows that the fluorescence recovers dramatically when isoniazid concentration increases. And the (F-F 0)/F 0 is proportional to isoniazid concentration ranging from 0.01 to 1 μM and 1–25 μM (Fig. 4B). The regression equations are (F-F 0)/F 0 = 0.861 + 0.808C (μM) (R2 = 0.991) and (F-F 0)/F 0 = 1.592 + 0.118C (μM) (R2 = 0.992), respectively. The LOD is calculated to be 3.3 nM (S/N = 3). In contrast, the MnO2-based single nanoquencher system was also used to detect isoniazid. Fig. 4C shows that the fluorescence of DAP-MnO2 system recovers gradually as isoniazid concentration increases, and (F-F 0)/F 0 displays two good linear relationships to isoniazid concentration from 0.1 to 5 μM and 5–100 μM with the LOD of 0.05 μM (Fig. 5 D). The regression equations are as follows: (F-F 0)/F 0 = 0.0466 + 0.177C (μM) (R2 = 0.992) and (F-F 0)/F 0 = 0.981 + 0.00836C (μM) (R2 = 0.996). These results indicate that the cascade amplification strategy based on double quenchers demonstrates higher sensitivity towards isoniazid. Compared with those reported methods, this method has wider linear range and lower LOD [31,32,34,35] (Table 1 ). Moreover, the LOD of this amplification strategy containing dual nanoquenchers is two orders of magnitude lower than those of other fluorescence methods based on single nanoquencher [21,27]. The reproducibility of this assay was investigated by six repetitive detection of isoniazid. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 2.68% for 0.5 μM isoniazid and 3.07% for 5 μM isoniazid. The results demonstrate the satisfactory reproducibility of the designed assay.

To examine the selectivity of this strategy, control experiments were carried out using possible interferences coexisting in pharmaceutical preparations including cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+), anions (Cl-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, SO4 2-), histidine, lysine, alanine, glucose, lactose, sucrose, starch, and urea were studied. As presented in Fig. 5, isoniazid induces obvious change for (F-F 0)/F 0 while other interfering substances have negligible effects on (F-F 0)/F 0. These results show that this strategy exhibits good selectivity for isoniazid detection.

To evaluate the practical application of strategy, isoniazid in the commercially available isoniazid tablet (100 mg per tablet) was analyzed. The obtained results were statistically compared with those obtained by HPLC method. As shown in Table 2 , the data show that both the results obtained by these two methods are consistent with the label claim. In addition, the calculated t- and F-values are less than the tabulated ones, confirming accuracy and precision at 95% confidence limits.

In general, a highly sensitive fluorescence method based on target-triggered cascade amplification reaction coupled with dual nanoquenchers has been successfully designed to detect isoniazid. Both MnO2 and AuNPs can quench the fluorescence of DAP via IFE. The addition of isoniazid can decompose the MnO2 to generate Mn2+, which induces the aggregation of AuNPs. Ascribed to the cascade amplified reaction, the fluorescence of DAP recovers remarkably. Compared with that based on MnO2 single nanoquencher, this strategy reduces the background signal and exhibits higher recovery efficiency, thus improving the sensitivity. The LOD of this proposed method is as low as 3.3 nM. The double quenching system is expected to construct more sensitive sensing platforms with high sensitivity.

Ya-Nan Zuo: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yinghui Xia: Formal analysis, Validation. Yanyu Li: Visualization. Jing Sun: Software. Xian-En Zhao: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Shuyun Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is kindly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22076097 and 22176109), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020MB066), and the Qinghai Special Project of Innovation Platform for Basic Conditions of Scientific Research of China (No. 2022-ZJ-Y18).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121234.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
